# legit-biz-club

The workspace layer of oakridge: multi-agent collaboration over a shared
artifact, built on jig (agent kit) and consumed by kbbl (operator
surface).

**Status:** v1 build complete. All five phases (foundation, incremental
coordination, convergence, evals + memory commit, study harness) shipped
on `main` across PRs #19, #23, and #24. Workstream D ‚Äî actually running
the v1 study ‚Äî is research output and lives outside this package.

## Shape

Python library; no CLI. Consumers (kbbl, study scripts) call the Python
API directly. See `README.md` for the per-module layout under
`src/legit_biz_club/` ‚Äî core, composition, coordination, eval, memory,
study, adapters.

## Dependencies

- `jig[anthropic,openai,ollama]` ‚Äî agent kit, multi-provider
- `pydantic`, `httpx`

The `adapters/kbbl/` module is a Python HTTP client against kbbl's TS
server. All cross-language calls go through it; do not hand-roll HTTP
elsewhere.

## Commands

```bash
uv sync                      # installs deps (jig is fetched from git)
uv run pytest                # runs the test suite
uv run pytest -q tests/study # study harness tests only (slower)
uv run ruff check            # lint
uv run ruff format           # format
uv run mypy                  # type-check
```

<!-- import: ../standards/core.md @ d1e1747157da -->
## Two gates before building

**Stop when uncertain.** Before introducing a pattern, dependency, file, or structural
decision with no precedent in these standards or the existing codebase ‚Äî stop and ask. A
wrong foundational choice is expensive to unwind; a question is cheap. This applies
especially to destructive file operations, schema and data-model decisions, new
dependencies, and changes to public interfaces.

**Data model before implementation.** No feature work begins until the domain model
exists as named types, derived from the real data source ‚Äî DB schema, API contract, CLI
output, upstream types. The model is not invented to fit code as it emerges. If you
cannot point at the source the model mirrors, you do not have a model yet. Improvised
data shapes metastasize: every consumer invents its own slice, arguments proliferate,
state scatters.

## Pipelines as the default

Express logic as composable pipelines of small named transforms ‚Äî each takes typed
input, returns typed output. If you cannot draw the data flow as a graph of typed nodes,
rethink the design.

- Each transform is independently testable ‚Äî pure or pure-ish, reusable across flows.
- Pipelines are fractal: a node can itself be a pipeline; a cycle is a nested subgraph.
- Use a loop when it is genuinely clearer ‚Äî early break, side effects, complex
  accumulation. Outside those three cases, default to the pipeline.

## Errors as values

A `Result` type (`Ok | Err`) is the connecting contract between pipeline stages. Every
fallible transform returns `Result`; the caller pattern-matches. Domain error types
carry trace context ‚Äî operation name, entity id, detail ‚Äî so a failure deep in a flow
can be traced to its path. `try`/`catch` lives only at IO boundaries: catch there,
convert to `Result`, hand it to the pipeline. (Concrete `Result` type: see the language
fragment.)

## Local primitives

Roll small primitives locally ‚Äî `Result`, domain error types, branded ids ‚Äî rather than
pull a library. They are a dozen lines, do not change, and importing them as a dependency
binds the project to someone else's release cadence for nothing in return. The language
fragments include drop-in definitions.

## Option handling

Represent absence with the language's nullable (`T | None`, `Option<T>`) ‚Äî never sentinel
strings, magic numbers, or empty collections as a stand-in for "not there". When two or
more values can be absent, prefer a chain of early-return guards over nested
conditionals; flat reads better than pyramidal.

## Derived values are transforms

A derived value is a pure named function of typed input. Call it a **selector** in UI
code, a **transform** everywhere else ‚Äî same idea, named for its setting. If a derivation
appears in two or more places, extract and name it. Consumers read derived values; they
never re-derive inline.

## Typing discipline

- Structured data always has a named type. Never a raw dict / untyped object /
  `Record<string, unknown>` for a known shape.
- A finite set of variants is a discriminated union ‚Äî a tag plus a typed payload per
  variant ‚Äî never a bare string tag beside an untyped bag.
- Domain identifiers get nominal/branded types (`UserId`, `OrderId`), not bare strings.

## Arguments

Three or more semantically related arguments ‚Üí group them into one named typed object.
Long pass-through argument lists ‚Äî arguments a function takes only to forward ‚Äî are a
layering smell, not a formatting one: before grouping them, ask whether the intermediate
function should exist at all.

## Testing

Test behavior, not implementation ‚Äî tests should survive refactors. Pure
transforms/selectors are the unit-test surface; side-effecting and IO code is tested at
its boundary. One clear assertion per test where practical; descriptive names.

## Naming

`snake_case` / `PascalCase` / `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` per the language's norm. Descriptive
over terse ‚Äî `user_count`, not `n`. Booleans read as questions ‚Äî `is_active`,
`has_permission`, `should_retry`.

<!-- import: ../standards/python.md @ d1e1747157da -->
## Tooling ‚Äî uv

`uv` for everything: `uv sync`, `uv add`, `uv run`. Never `pip`, `poetry`, or `conda`.
Commit `uv.lock`. `uv run` is the entry point for every command ‚Äî tests, lint, scripts ‚Äî
never bare `python` / `pytest`. Pin the interpreter in `.python-version` (3.12+, required
for the generic syntax below) and commit it.

## Result type

A project-local `result.py`:

```python
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class Ok[T]:
    value: T

@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class Err[E]:
    error: E

type Result[T, E] = Ok[T] | Err[E]
```

Fallible functions return `Result`; callers `match` on `Ok` / `Err`.

## Typing

- `from __future__ import annotations` at the top of every module.
- `X | None`, not `Optional[X]`; `X | Y`, not `Union`.
- `collections.abc` types in signatures ‚Äî `Sequence`, `Mapping`, `Iterable` ‚Äî over
  concrete `list` / `dict`.
- `pydantic.BaseModel` for data crossing a boundary; `@dataclass(frozen=True,
  slots=True)` for internal domain types; `TypedDict` for JSON-shaped payloads.
- Never a raw dict for structured data ‚Äî define a type.

## Quality

`ruff check`, `ruff format`, and `mypy` all clean. Every function fully annotated,
parameters and return. Prefer concrete types over `Any`; reaching for `Any` is a design
smell.

## Imports

Three groups, blank-line separated: standard library, third-party, local. Absolute
imports from the project root. No wildcard imports. Prefer importing the symbols you use
(`from x import foo`) over importing the module wholesale, unless the module is small and
referenced pervasively (`import os`, `import typing as t`).

## Python naming

The language-agnostic rules in `core.md` apply. Python-specific: a single leading
underscore marks a member as private (`_internal_state`). Do not use double-underscore
name mangling (`__private`) as "more private" ‚Äî it exists to avoid attribute collisions
in inheritance hierarchies, not as an access modifier, and using it that way misleads
readers about intent.

## Python testing

`pytest` is the runner. Tests are module-level functions, not methods on a class. State
varies through fixtures ‚Äî including factory fixtures for objects that need many shapes
across tests ‚Äî not `setUp` / `tearDown`. Descriptive test names; one clear assertion per
test where practical.
